title: Education policy, space and the city: markets and the (in)visibility of race
abstract: Click to increase image sizeClick to decrease image size Notes 1. This is my emphasis. Gulson appears to prefer to avoid the notion of determinism in his discussion. 2. Incidentally, Gulson highlights the political use of measurement or demography as an illustration of the technologies of government in representing the city (Sydney in this case) and schooling, but then routinely (and unproblematically) draws upon the statistics of the State to illustrate his points about the limited nature of multiculturalism and ‘mixed’ neighbourhoods in East Vancouver or to demonstrate the levels of socio-economic disadvantage in Inner Sydney.
authors_text: Taylor, Chris; McLeod, Julie; Butler, Tim; Vincent, Carol
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
